coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of cellulose nanofibrils modified with carbon dots-graft-polyacrylamide/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel for simultaneous adsorption and detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xuefeng
Peng, Junwen
Huang, Huijian
Qi, Xinmiao
Zhang, Ning
Wang, Yuanlan
Qiao, Jianzheng
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: To address the pollution of antibiotics in water resources, a novel cellulose nanofibrils modified with carbon dots-graft-polyacrylamide/Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 composite hydrogel (CZCH) was synthesized in this study for simultaneous adsorption and detection of tetracycline (TC). The morphology, structure, fluorescent properties, and adsorption and sensing capabilities of CZCH were comprehensively characterized using various analytical techniques. The results demonstrated the CZCH's remarkable adsorption capacity for TC that reached a maximum of 810.36 mg/g. The Langmuir model provided an excellent fit for the adsorption isotherm, while the pseudo-secondary model accurately described the adsorption kinetics. Thermodynamic analysis revealed that the adsorption process was characterized by an endothermic reaction. Impressively, the CZCH maintained 85.6% retention of its initial adsorption capacity even after undergoing five consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles. Moreover, the synthesized CZCH exhibited blue fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield of 5.1%, enabling selective and efficient detection of TC with a low limit of detection of 0.09 μg/L. Furthermore, the mechanism underlying the adsorption and detection of TC by CZCH was elucidated. Cost analysis confirmed that CZCH had a significantly lower cost of CNY 0.07 per gram of TC removal compared to activated carbon, which costs CNY 5.74. Thus, this study presents a sustainable approach for synthesizing a low-cost and multifunctional adsorbent that can be employed for the adsorption and detection of antibiotics.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of cellulose nanofibrils modified with carbon dots-graft-polyacrylamide/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel for simultaneous adsorption and detection of tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the pollution of antibiotics in water resources, a novel cellulose nanofibrils modified with carbon dots-graft-polyacrylamide/Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 composite hydrogel (CZCH) was synthesized in this study for simultaneous adsorption and detection of tetracycline (TC). The morphology, structure, fluorescent properties, and adsorption and sensing capabilities of CZCH were comprehensively characterized using various analytical techniques. The results demonstrated the CZCH's remarkable adsorption capacity for TC that reached a maximum of 810.36 mg/g. The Langmuir model provided an excellent fit for the adsorption isotherm, while the pseudo-secondary model accurately described the adsorption kinetics. Thermodynamic analysis revealed that the adsorption process was characterized by an endothermic reaction. Impressively, the CZCH maintained 85.6% retention of its initial adsorption capacity even after undergoing five consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles. Moreover, the synthesized CZCH exhibited blue fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield of 5.1%, enabling selective and efficient detection of TC with a low limit of detection of 0.09 μg/L. Furthermore, the mechanism underlying the adsorption and detection of TC by CZCH was elucidated. Cost analysis confirmed that CZCH had a significantly lower cost of CNY 0.07 per gram of TC removal compared to activated carbon, which costs CNY 5.74. Thus, this study presents a sustainable approach for synthesizing a low-cost and multifunctional adsorbent that can be employed for the adsorption and detection of antibiotics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the increase in water contamination caused by the presence of antibiotics, originating from both human and animal sources has emerged as a significant environmental concern [1]. Tetracycline (TC), a popular antibiotic, is extensively used in human therapy, veterinary medicine, and agricultural practices because of its cost-effectiveness [2]. Following administration, a considerable amount of TC is excreted in its active form and ultimately enters the environment through human and animal urine and feces. In certain medical and pharmaceutical wastewater streams, the concentration of TC can reach remarkably high levels, ranging from 100 to 500 mg/L [3]. Various methods, such as adsorption [4], ozone oxidation [5], microbial degradation [6] and photocatalytic degradation [7] have been employed for removing TC from water sources. Among them, adsorption is considered as an effective method due to its practicality, cost-effectiveness, efficiency, and simplicity. However, adsorbents such as activated carbon (AC), molecular sieves, silica gels, resins, and microbial flocculants, are associated with high production costs and thus have limited widespread application [8]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop affordable and efficient adsorbent materials.

Hydrogel is a highly promising adsorbent for TC removal due to its distinctive physicochemical properties, such as hydrophilicity, modifiability, environmental friendliness, and exceptional adsorption capacity. Zhuang et al. developed a three-dimensional alginate-based metal organic framework hydrogel as an adsorbent for TC removal [9]. Ni et al. synthesized a carbon nanotube/graphene-based alginate interpenetrating hydrogel for removing antibiotic pollutants and demonstrated its good TC adsorption capacity of 84.28 mg/g [10]. Li et al. prepared a double-network hydrogel by combining chitosan-cellulose composite as the rigid network and polyacrylamide as the flexible network and highlighted the impressive adsorption capacity and efficient TC uptake ability of the hydrogel [11]. In recent years, cellulose nanofibrils (CN) have garnered substantial attention owing to their exceptional inherent properties, high abundance in nature, facile modifiability, biodegradability, and cost-effectiveness [12]. CN also exhibit high specific surface area, elevated aspect ratio, and remarkable strength, and thus are exceedingly attractive for the production of cost-effective and efficient hydrogels. As a result, CN emerges as a promising and viable choice for hydrogel fabrication.

Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), known as an appealing material, has garnered significant attention as an adsorbent for TC removal from wastewater due to its outstanding properties, including a large specific surface area [13]. Yang et al. synthesized Fe-doped ZIF-8, and demonstrated its excellent TC adsorption capacity of 867.00 mg/g [14]. Despite the high adsorption capacity demonstrated by ZIF-8 and its mixture, the primary challenge in practical applications lies in their powdered form. The powdered form of ZIF-8 is difficult to prepare, has poor distribution, and tends to aggregate easily, thereby hindering the effective and practical application of ZIF-8 adsorbents [15]. This issue can be addressed by immobilizing ZIF-8 in an appropriate matrix, such as hydrogel, due to its natural hydrophilicity and 3D porous structure.

Carbon dots (CD), categorized as carbon-based nanomaterials, offer numerous advantageous features, including exceptional photoluminescence properties, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, cost-effectiveness, the ability to facilitate targeted interactions with other molecules, and environmental friendliness [16,17]. Numerous reports have highlighted the utility of CD as valuable fluorescent sensors for TC. Thara et al. successfully synthesized N-doped CD, which demonstrated exceptional sensitivity and selectivity toward TC and reported its limit of detection (LOD) for TC to be 56 nM [18]. In a similar vein, Yan et al. synthesized yellow-green luminescent CD capable of detecting TC in aqueous solutions and exhibited a favorable linear relationship between luminescence intensity and TC concentration with a LOD of 6.0 μM [19]. However, as fluorescent sensors, CD exhibit weak stability and a tendency to aggregate. These challenges can be addressed by incorporating CD into a hydrogel.

Thus, in this study, a novel CN modified with CD/ZIF-8 composite hydrogel (CZCH) was synthesized for simultaneous adsorption and detection of TC. In addition, the morphology, structure, fluorescent properties, and adsorption and sensing behaviors of the synthesized CZCH were thoroughly characterized. Based on these experimental results, a mechanistic model was proposed to rationalize the high-efficiency adsorption and sensitive sensing of TC by CZCH. Finally, a cost-estimation analysis was conducted to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of using CZCH.

The synthesis process involved the dispersion of bleached wood pulp (2.5 g) in 300 mL of deionized water, followed by the sequential addition of NaBr (0.3 g), aqueous NaOCl solution (20 g, 10 wt%), and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical (TEMPO, 0.05 g). The pH of the mixture was changed to 10. The TEMPO oxidation was allowed to proceed for 5 h before being quenched by the addition of ethanol. The resulting mixture was homogenized using a M110P microfluidizer for six cycles and subsequently washed with deionized water until the pH of the suspension was 7. Finally, the suspension was subjected to dialysis against deionized water for five days using a dialysis bag, leading to the production of CN suspension.

CD were synthesized using a mixture containing citric acid (4.2 g), ethylenediamine (12.75 mL), and ultrapure water (100 mL). The mixture was then introduced into the high-pressure reactor and subjected to a thermal treatment at a temperature of 200 °C for 5 h. The obtained product was allowed to cool to 25 °C and subsequently subjected to dialysis for 5 days.

0.5 wt% suspension of CN (10.0 g) was blended with 10 mL of deionized water and continuously stirred until a homogenous mixture was formed. Then, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.10 g), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.12 g), and CD solution (1.3 g, 0.5 wt%) were subsequently added to the mixture sequentially, and the resulting mixture was stirred continuously for 12 h at room temperature under dark conditions. The resultant product, identified as CN-CD, was subjected to dialysis for 5 days to remove any impurities.

ZIF-8 was synthesized by separately dissolving zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.17 g) and 2-methylimidazole (22.7 g) in deionized water (8 and 80 g), respectively. The obtained solutions were promptly combined in a 300 mL glass beaker and stirred for 5 min at 25 °C. This formed a white powdery solid, which was centrifuged for 10 min at a speed of 1100 rpm, followed by purification using two washing cycles with 30 mL of deionized water. The purified ZIF-8 powder was subsequently dried under vacuum in an oven overnight at 80 °C to remove any moisture and impurities.

0.25 g of acrylamide (AM) was added in a four-mouth flask equipped with a thermometer, condenser tube, and nitrogen line. Subsequently, varying amounts of CN-CD suspension ranging from 1.0 to 8.0 g were added over a 30-min duration. Following this, ZIF-8 (0.1 g) and N′,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 0.02 g) were added to the mixture and allowed to stir for 60 min. Next, N2 gas was purged through the solution for 1 h to remove dissolved oxygen, following which potassium persulfate (PPS, 0.015 g) was introduced. The reaction mixture was heated to 75 °C for 30 min for polymerization. The resulting hydrogels were designated as CZCH-1 through CZCH-8 based on the quantity of CN-CD suspension used. For comparative analysis, control hydrogel was prepared in parallel but without CD and ZIF-8, and then was labeled as CH-4. These hydrogels were immersed in deionized water for 24 h to remove residual chemicals.

A series of 250 mL conical flasks, containing a 100 mL of TC solution, were used for the batch adsorption experiments. The conical flasks were agitated in a thermodynamic water bath shaker operating at 200 rpm. Adsorption experiments were executed in the pH range of 2–10 (initial concentration = 100 mg/L; contact time = 24 h; temperature = 25 °C). Kinetic experiments were conducted at contact times ranging from 30 to 240 min (initial concentration = 100 mg/L; pH = 8; temperature = 25 °C). Equilibrium experiments were performed at initial TC concentrations from 20 to 100 mg/L (temperature = 25 °C; pH = 8; contact time = 24 h). Additional equilibrium experiments were executed for thermodynamic analysis at temperatures of 15 ℃, 25 ℃, 35 ℃, 45 ℃, and 55 ℃ (initial concentration = 100 mg/L; pH = 8; contact time = 24 h). All experiments were set up in three parallels for error analysis.

Microstructures of the samples were observed using a Hitachi S4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM). The elemental compositions were characterized using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) equipped on the SEM. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Rigaku-D/max 2550 PC (Japan) diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Nicolet iS10 spectrometer) was used to analyze the functional groups of the samples prepared by the attenuated total reflectance technique. The surface area was determined using a BET instrument (Quantachrome Instruments v11.02). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher, American). The excitation and emission spectra were examined using a JASCO FP-6500 (Japan). Both the recording of the UV–vis absorption spectra and the quantification of TC concentration at λmax = 357 nm were performed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2550, Japan).

As presented in Fig. 1 , a novel synthetic route for CZCH was used, in which CD and ZIF-8 were introduced as new constituents compared to the CN-based hydrogel. CN were initially obtained through TEMPO-mediated oxidation of wood pulp. CD were subsequently synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine. The synthesized CN were bound onto CD via EDC/NHS-assisted graft copolymerization. Finally, the CZCH was produced via radical polymerization. The addition of CN facilitated the construction of a 3D porous structure that offered more sorption sites, whereas CD provided a rapid fluorescence response to TC and exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity. These unique structural features attributed to the effective TC adsorption and detection capabilities of the resultant CZCH.

The morphological characteristics of four samples, including CN, CD, CN-CD, and CZCH-4, were investigated using SEM. SEM analysis of CN (as presented in Fig. 2 a) revealed the presence of nanoscale rod-like and wire-like cellulose fibers [20]. The synthesized CD exhibited a spherical morphology (Fig. S1). The high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. S1) revealed distinct lattice fringes with a lattice parameter of 0.2 nm, corresponding to the in-plane lattice fringe (100) facet of grapheme [17]. The coarse morphology of CN-CD (as illustrated in Fig. S2) was attributed to the presence of CD on the CN surface [21]. In addition, CZCH-4 demonstrated a 3D porous architecture (Fig. 2b), with the largest specific surface area among the prepared CZCHs (Fig. S3). Moreover, we performed elemental component analysis of CZCH-4 using the EDS method and observed a uniform distribution of C, O, N, and Zn (Fig. S4). This finding confirmed the successful incorporation of ZIF-8 into the CZCH-4.

The crystallinity of four samples, namely CN, CN-CD, ZIF-8, and CZCH-4, was investigated using XRD patterns (as shown in Fig. 2c). Both CN and CN-CD exhibited two peaks at 16.2° and 22.7°, respectively, which were associated with the (1_10) and (200) crystalline planes of cellulose I in its crystalline form [22]. The formation of these peaks suggested that the grafting of CD onto CN had a negligible impact on the cellulose crystal structure of CN [23]. In addition, the XRD patterns of ZIF-8 showed diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 10.4°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, 18.0°, 24.5°, and 26.7°, corresponding to the (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (233), and (134) crystalline planes, respectively [24]. However, in the case of CZCH-4, the XRD peaks originating from CN-CD and ZIF-8 exhibited reduced intensities, probably due to the destruction of their crystalline structures because of the free-radical polymerization of CN-CD and the wrapping of ZIF-8 [25]. The CZCH-4 exhibited two halos, between 25° and 30° and between 40° and 45°, indicating an amorphous structure. Free radical polymerization reaction caused a hydrogel network to form, which consisted of knots and porous walls. Most parts inside the CZCH-4 were pores filled with the liquid. The diffuse scattering of the liquid system, which consisted of water in this case, caused the hydrogel to be amorphous, as also described in previous research [26].

The chemical structure of CN, CN-CD, ZIF-8, and CZCH-4 was analyzed using FTIR analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2d. The FTIR spectra of CN and CN-CD exhibited one typical peak at 1050 cm−1, attributed to the C − O − C stretching vibration [27]. In addition, one new peak observed at 1650 cm−1 in CN-CD was due to the –CO–NH– stretching vibration [23]. This observation suggested that CD were grafted onto CN via a chemical bond of –CO–NH–. The FTIR spectrum of ZIF-8 revealed three characteristic peaks at 1310, 756, and 693 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching of imidazole cyclophenyl C–H and C = C, Zn–O stretching, and Zn–N stretching, respectively [28,29]. The FTIR spectrum of CZCH-4 showed that the typical peaks of ZIF-8 and CN-CD became less intense, suggesting that ZIF-8 was encapsulated into CZCH-4 and that CN-CD participated in the free-radical polymerization process to produce CZCH-4 [30].

Optical properties of CZCH-4 were studied using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2e, one absorption band located at 280 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [31]. In addition, Fig. 2e displayed the optimal photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra, with an optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an optimal emission wavelength of 480 nm. As shown in Fig. 2f, the excitation wavelength of the CZCH-4 ranged from 335 to 365 nm, and its corresponding fluorescence peak gradually shifted from 473 to 495 nm. This observation was attributed to the effect of the surface state on the band gap of CD [32].

The fluorescence performance of the prepared CZCHs is depicted in Fig. 2g. As shown in the figure, all CZCHs displayed blue fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp irradiation, with their fluorescent brightness increasing initially and decreasing subsequently with the increase in CN-CD content [33]. Interestingly, CZCH-4 displayed the highest fluorescent brightness, characterized by an absolute quantum yield (AQY) of 5.1%.

CZCH-4 toxicity was evaluated in NIH/3T3 cells using a CCK-8 assay. Fig. S5 shows the cell viability results, which indicate minimal cytotoxicity (99%) of both synthesized CZCH-4 and CH-4, even at high concentrations (200 mg/L). Thus, CZCH-4 was found to be non-toxic to the NIH/3T3 cells, and it did not release any cytotoxic compounds. Fig. S6 displays the results of the live/dead staining assay conducted after 48 h on the synthesized CZCH-4 for NIH/3T3 cells. As shown in the figure, live cells emitted blue fluorescence, while dead cells emitted red fluorescence. Importantly, the images exhibited almost imperceptible dead cells. These results highlighted that the synthesized CZCH-4 had no toxicity, indicating it to be highly suitable for water treatment [34].

The influence of three key parameters, i.e., pH, temperature (T), and initial TC concentration (C0), on the equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) of CZCH-4 was investigated. The obtained findings are presented in Fig. 3 a-c, which provide a visual representation of the relationship between these parameters and Qe.

As shown in Fig. 3d, the Qe of CZCH-4 increased with the increase in pH from 2 to 8 but decreased in the pH range of 8–10. The pH-dependent adsorption behavior was attributed to the charges carried by CZCH-4 and TC [35]. The pHpzc of the CZCH-4 was 8.5 (Fig. S7), indicating that CZCH-4 was positively charged at pH values lower than pHpzc and negatively charged at pH values higher than pHpzc. TC has three pKa values of 3.3, 7.7, and 9.7 and consequently exists as cationic, neutral, and anionic species at pH ranges < 3.3, 3.3～9.7, and above 7.7, respectively [36]. The molecular structure of TC was provided in Fig. S8. Notably, the low adsorption capacity was observed at pH 2 because of electrostatic repulsion. Conversely, the higher adsorption capacity at pH 8 could be attributed to the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged CZCH-4 and the negatively charged TC [37]. Therefore, a pH of 8 was used in the subsequent adsorption experiments.

We also examined the adsorption capacity of CZCH-4 toward TC at varying temperatures. As depicted in Fig. 3e, the adsorption capacity of CZCH-4 gradually increased when the temperature increased to 15 ℃, 25 ℃, 35 ℃, 45 ℃, and 55 ℃. This finding indicated the endothermic nature of the adsorption process [38]. The thermodynamic parameters, namely Gibbs free energy (ΔG), enthalpy (ΔH), and entropy (ΔS), can be determined by linear regression analysis of ln(Qe/Ce) versus 1/T and utilizing Van't Hoff equations (Fig. S9). ΔH was determined to be 9.65 kJ/mol, indicating the endothermic nature of the adsorption process. In addition, a positive ΔS value of 33.73 J/(mol K) demonstrated a favorable affinity of TC toward CZCH-4. Moreover, the ΔG values ranging from − 0.07 to − 1.42 KJ/mol verified the spontaneous characteristics of the TC adsorption process onto CZCH-4 [39]. Hence, a temperature of 25 ℃ was chosen for further analysis so to facilitate practical applications.

We investigated the adsorption behavior of CZCH-4 and CH-4 toward TC and presented the results in Fig. 3f. As shown in the figure, the Qe of CZCH-4 and CH-4 increased with the increase in the initial concentration of TC. In addition, the adsorption ability for TC by CZCH-4 was superior to that by CH-4, indicating enhanced adsorption performance of CZCH-4 due to the incorporation of CD and ZIF-8 [40]. These experimental adsorption data were modeled using the Langmuir and Freundlich models. The plots are given in Fig. 3h and 3i, while the values of parameters are summarized in Table S1. Langmuir model fitted better for TC adsorption by CZCH-4, indicating the specific homogeneous sites in the adsorbent [41,42]. The maximum adsorption capacity of CZCH-4 was determined to be 810.36 mg/g. Compared with other adsorbents presented in Table 1 , CZCH-4 demonstrated great potential as an effective adsorbent for the removal of TC.

The present study investigated the time-dependent adsorption behavior of TC onto CZCH-4 and CH-4, and the results are depicted in Fig. 3g. As shown in the figure, the adsorption capacity of both adsorbents increased with the increase in contact time, with CZCH-4 exhibiting a higher adsorption capacity than CH-4 [51]. The adsorption kinetics was further analyzed using the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models. The plots are given in Fig. 3j and 3 k, while the values of parameters are summarized in Table S2. Pseudo-second-order model fitted better for TC adsorption by CZCH-4, which suggested that the adsorption mechanism was primarily controlled by chemical adsorption [52].

Five consecutive adsorption–desorption experiments were carried out to evaluate the reusability of CZCH-4 [53–55]. Prior to each cycle, CZCH-4 was pre-treated by soaking it in 0.1 M MeOH for 3 h, followed by rinsing it five times with deionized water. Fig. 4 a illustrates that the adsorption capacity of CZCH-4 remained 85.6% higher than the initial value throughout the five cycles. SEM, FTIR, and XRD confirmed the structural integrity of CZCH-4 after five cycles. As shown in the SEM images (Fig. S10), the structural morphology of CZCH-4 after five cycles was consistent with that of the as-synthesized CZCH-4, indicating that its 3D porous structure was retained [56]. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4b) revealed that the chemical groups of CZCH-4 after five cycles were similar to those of the original CZCH-4, further confirming its structural integrity [57]. The XRD analysis (Fig. 4c) also confirmed that the crystal structure of CZCH-4 remained stable [58]. All these results suggest that CZCH-4 possess excellent regeneration and reusability characteristics.

The viability of employing CZCH-4 as an adsorbent for TC adsorption in real water was also evaluated. For this, we collected an actual water sample from the wastewater treatment facility at The Second Xiangya Hospital of Central South University. The quality parameters of the sample were analyzed and then documented in Table S3. The sample was subjected to adsorption tests under optimized conditions, i.e., pH of 8, TC concentration of 5 mg/L, contact time of 24 h, and temperature of 25 ℃. The Qe of the real water sample was determined to be 1.96 mg/g, which corresponded to 87.8% of the Qe obtained for CZCH-4 in synthetic water. Although the competitive adsorption of coexisting pollutants reduced the adsorption capacity, the results confirmed the practicality and efficacy of CZCH-4.

The selectivity of the CZCH-4 sensor toward TC is a crucial feature for its effective detection ability [59]. This selectivity of CZCH-4 was evaluated by investigating its fluorescence behavior under 365 nm UV light in the presence of various antibiotics, including TC, oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), minocycline (MINO), doxycycline (DOXY), sulfadiazine (SD), chloramphenicol (CAP), ciprofloxacin (CIP), enrofloxacin (ENR), and ofloxacin (OFX). As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CZCH-4 displayed rapid and complete fluorescence quenching only in the presence of TC derivatives, particularly TC. A similar trend was also seen in the AQY of CZCH-4 (Fig. 5b). As shown, the fluorescence quenching induced by TC was the most pronounced, attributed to the precise overlap between the absorption band of TC and the excitation band of CZCH-4 (Fig. S11) [60]. The results confirmed that the prepared CZCH-4 had good selectivity ability for detecting TC.

Investigation of various detection conditions including temperature, pH, and response time is essential to establish a reliable and stable platform for TC detection. Fig. S12 demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of CZCH-4 remained stable even at a temperature of 45 °C. Nevertheless, the fluorescence response of CZCH-4 to TC increased with rising temperature. For convenience, subsequent detection experiments were conducted at 25 °C. Fig. S13 demonstrates a sharp increase in fluorescence intensity of CZCH-4 as the pH value varied from 2 to 4, with minimal change observed within the pH range of 4–10. Moreover, CZCH-4 exhibited a significant fluorescence response to TC at pH 8, establishing it as the optimal pH value. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of CZCH-4 to TC rapidly increased within 6 min and then stabilized, as shown in Fig. S14. Consequently, a response time of 6 min was selected for subsequent detection experiments.

The feasibility of utilizing CZCH-4 for detecting TC in actual water was assessed. For this, two water samples were gathered from a running water pipe and an artificial lake located within the campus of Central South University of Forestry and Technology. CZCH-4 and ICP-MS did not detect any TC in these two water samples. In such a case, the standard addition approach can be employed. As shown in Table S4, the standard addition recovery of TC was 98.77%–101.12%, and the standard deviation was lower than 3%. These outcomes confirmed the practicality and efficacy of CZCH-4 in determining TC.

The adsorption mechanism of CZCH-4 was examined by utilizing FTIR spectroscopy, and the results are presented in Fig. 7 a. As shown in the figure, the peak intensity showed a decrease, which was associated with N–H and O–H stretching vibrations at 3340 cm−1, following the adsorption of TC [70]. In addition, an increase in peak intensity related to C–N stretching vibration at 1395 cm−1 was also observed [71]. The above changes confirmed that –OH and –NH2 were effective for TC adsorption.

The adsorption mechanism of CZCH-4 was further examined using XPS. As shown in the XPS results in Fig. 7b, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Zn2p peaks were obtained at 285, 399, 531, and 1021 eV, respectively [72]. O1s XPS spectrum of CZCH-4 (Fig. 7c) demonstrated three component peaks at 530.6 eV (Zn–O), 531.3 eV (C = O), and 532.4 eV (C–O) [73]. After adsorption, these peaks shifted to 530.5, 531.2, and 532.3 eV, respectively, indicating hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl group and TC [74]. N1s XPS spectra of CZCH-4 before and after TC adsorption are shown in Fig. 7d. The peaks at 398.8, 399.3, and 400.1 eV were assigned to –N=, –NH2 and –NH3 +, respectively [75]. The –NH3 + peak's intensity increased after TC adsorption, confirming electrostatic interactions between–NH3 + and TC. The above results confirmed that TC was attracted by CZCH-4 via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions.

TC detection mechanism of CZCH-4 was investigated using the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The results are presented in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8a, the absorption band of TC and the fluorescence excitation band of CZCH-4 exhibited a significant degree of overlap [76]. Inner filter effect (IFE) usually occurs between the quencher and the fluorophore because the quencher absorbs the excitation light of the fluorophore in the detection system. Hence, IFE may account for the detection of TC by CZCH-4 [77]. In addition, the average lifetimes of CZCH-4 with and without TC were determined to be 4.87 and 4.91 ns, respectively (Fig. 8b-d). Remarkably, the average lifetimes of CZCH-4 prior to and following the introduction of TC had small changes. Thus, we confirmed that IFE was the primary mechanism through which CZCH-4 detected TC.

Based on the obtained results, Fig. 9 illustrates the adsorption and detection mechanism of TC on CZCH. Initially, TC underwent electrostatic adsorption on the surface of CZCH that was facilitated by the presence of amino groups. Subsequently, the porous structure of CZCH acted as a mass transfer channel, enabling rapid movement of TC from the surface to the interior. During this movement, TC underwent adsorption via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. Simultaneously, CD were encapsulated within the porous structures of CZCH, serving as the fluorescence probe. The electrostatic attractions led to the rapid adsorption of TC onto CD, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Thus, the CZCH demonstrated high-efficiency detection of TC based on the IFE.

The economic viability of an adsorbent is a critical determinant in assessing its practical application [78]. Thus, the cost-effectiveness of CZCH-4 was evaluated via a cost-estimation analysis. Here the costs involved in each stage of its preparation and the total cost of production were estimated in accordance with the established literature procedures [79]. The results are reported in Table S5. The production cost of 1 kg of CZCH-4 was found to be CNY 57.67, in comparison to the price of AC available at CNY 253.00 per kg from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (China). Here, CZCH-4 demonstrated superior cost efficiency compared to AC. Moreover, the cost-effectiveness of CZCH-4 and AC in removing 1 kg of TC could be assessed, as detailed in Table S6. Note that, at CNY 0.07 and CNY 5.74, CZCH-4 displayed a significantly lower cost per gram of TC removal than AC, owing to its superior adsorption capacity. Therefore, based on its favorable environmental and economic attributes, CZCH-4 was identified as a low-cost adsorbent for TC removal.

In this study, a 3D porous structured CZCH was successfully synthesized using CN, ZIF-8, and CD. The adsorption isotherms and kinetics analysis demonstrated that TC adsorption in CZCH adhered to the Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models, resulting in enhanced maximum adsorption capacity up to 810.36 mg/g. Furthermore, CZCH exhibited an impressive detection performance, featuring a low detection limit of 0.09 μg/L and two analytical linear ranges of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and 1–300 mg/L, enabling sensitive and selective detection of TC. CZCH demonstrated ease of separation from aqueous solutions post-adsorption while maintaining excellent adsorption capacities even after undergoing five adsorption–desorption cycles. TC adsorption was primarily governed by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, while TC detection relied on the fluorescence quenching of CZCH induced by IFE. Cost analysis indicated that CZCH was significantly more cost-effective compared to the CA. Thus, CZCH exhibited immense potential for the removal of TC, owing to its affordability, effectiveness, and reusability.
